title: Tracking Interpersonal Effectiveness Skills Use

pages.text:
175
From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. Permis-
sion to photocopy this worksheet is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use only (see copyright page for details). Purchasers may
download a larger version of this worksheet from www.guilford.com/dbt-worksheets.
Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 5 (I. E. Handouts 5, 6, 7)
Tracking Interpersonal Effectiveness Skills Use
Due Date: Name: Week Starting:
Fill out this sheet whenever you practice your interpersonal skills and whenever you have an oppor-
tunity to practice, even if you don’t (or almost don’t) do anything to practice. Write on the back of this
sheet if you need more room.
Prompting event for my problem: Who did what to whom? What led up to what? Objectives in Situation (What results I want): Relationship Issue (How I want the other person to feel about me): Self-Respect Issue (How I want to feel about myself):
My priorities in this situation: Rate priorities 1 (most important), 2 (second most important),
or 3 (least important). Objectives Relationship Self-respect
Imbalances and conflicts in priorities that made it hard to be effective in this situation:
What I said or did in the situation: (Describe and check below.)
DEAR MAN (Getting what I want): Described situation? Mindful? Expressed feelings/opinions? Broken record? Asserted? Ignored attacks? Reinforced? Appeared confident?
GIVE (Keeping the relationship): Negotiated? Gentle? Interested? No threats? Validated? No attacks? Easy manner? No judgments?
FAST (Keeping my respect for myself): Fair? Stuck to values? (No) Apologies? Truthful?
How effective was the interaction?

ourspace_kindness_pass.title: Tracking Interpersonal Effectiveness Skills Use

main_doability_needs: bed-level or seated options by default
oxygen/breathing, pain, vertigo, fatigue, mobility, and recovery time respected
ADHD/autism supports: transitions, low-stimulation starts, tiny visible steps
partial completion and stopping before a crash count as success
no shame, no body blame, no pressure to push through pain or dysphoria
ADHD transition support before task demands
caregiver burnout recovery treated as care, not avoidance
protected rest, boundaries, decompression, sensory needs, and people-pleasing checks
no assumption that Jasper has unlimited caregiver capacity

household_rules: no hour block may hold more than two tasks/tools
skills may be used as one-breath or one-field versions
survival days load critical care first and skip optional worksheets
rest, boundaries, decompression, and asking for help count as care
skills are for support, not grades

universal_kindness_rules: This is a support tool, not a test.
One line, one checkbox, one breath, or one tiny step counts.
Skipping a skill because the body is unsafe, exhausted, dysregulated, or overloaded is allowed.
Do not stack this with more than one other task inside the same hour.
Survival-day version always wins over perfection.
william_accessibility_adaptations: Use bed-level, seated, voice-note, or caregiver-assisted versions when needed.
Avoid pain escalation, respiratory strain, unsafe transfers, and dysphoria-triggering language.
Break worksheets into one-field chunks; stopping before a crash is success.
jasper_accessibility_adaptations: Start with transition support before expecting focus.
Protect caregiver burnout recovery; rest and boundaries are valid completion.
Use body-doubling, timers, low-stimulation versions, and one-minute starts.
tiny_steps: Open the file without requiring completion.
Fill one field, circle one option, or read one prompt.
Stop, save, or print; partial completion counts.

routine_integration.start_the_day_use: eligible as a rotating daily skill, never more than one DBT/ADHD skill in an hour block
routine_integration.recommended_block: after body check or before decompression
